name: Junctional Rhythm
identifyBy: Narrow complex rhythm at 40–60 bpm (AV node escape rate)
Absent P waves OR retrograde P waves (inverted in II, III, aVF)
If retrograde P: may appear before, during, or after QRS depending on timing
QRS narrow — conduction through ventricles is normal
Escape rhythm — appears when sinus node fails or AV conduction is impaired

measurements.pWaves: Absent or retrograde (inverted II, III, aVF)
measurements.prInterval: Short (<120ms) if P before QRS; negative RP if P after QRS

management: Treat the underlying cause — junctional rhythm is an escape mechanism
If symptomatic (hypotension, altered consciousness): Atropine 0.6mg IV
Identify cause: inferior MI, digoxin toxicity, increased vagal tone, post-cardiac surgery
Transcutaneous pacing if haemodynamically unstable and atropine fails
Do NOT suppress the junctional rhythm without an alternative pacemaker — it is keeping the heart going
pearls: Junctional rhythm is a protective escape mechanism — its suppression without pacemaker backup is dangerous
Digoxin toxicity is a classic cause — check serum digoxin level
Junctional rhythm after inferior MI: AV node ischaemia — usually transient and resolves
The position of the P wave relative to QRS defines high (P before QRS), mid (P in QRS), or low (P after QRS) nodal origin
differentials: Complete heart block with junctional escape
